posts.title: The Emir of Bukhara, 1911
posts.text:
The Emir of Bukhara, Alim Khan. Photographed by Sergey Prokudin-Gorsky in 1911, four years before the Russian Empire absorbed the emirate completely.

This is not a colorized photo. Prokudin-Gorsky shot three separate exposures through red, green, and blue filters onto a single glass plate. The three negatives were then projected through matching filters to produce a full-color image. In 1911.

Alim Khan was 31 here. He'd been emir for one year. He would rule for nine more before the Bolsheviks took Bukhara in 1920. He fled to Afghanistan with whatever gold he could carry and died in Kabul in 1944.

The silk robe, the jeweled sword, the palace behind him — all of it gone within a decade of this photo.

Sergey Prokudin-Gorsky, 1911. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
The Emir of Bukhara, 1911. Not colorized. Three separate exposures through red, green, and blue filters onto glass. Full color, 1911.

Alim Khan was 31. He'd been emir for one year. The Bolsheviks took Bukhara in 1920. He fled to Afghanistan with whatever gold he could carry. Died in Kabul, 1944.

The silk robe, the jeweled sword, the palace behind him — gone within a decade of this photo.

Sergey Prokudin-Gorsky. Library of Congress.

posts.title: The Grand Canal, Venice, 1895
posts.text:
The Grand Canal, Venice. Photochrom print, circa 1895. The colors aren't paint — they're lithographic tints applied chemically to a black-and-white negative. A process invented in Zurich in 1888.

The gondolas in this frame are working boats, not tourist rides. In 1895 Venice had about 10,000 gondolas handling daily transport. Today there are 400, all for tourists.

The buildings haven't moved. But the water has. Venice floods about 60 times a year now, up from 10 times a year when this photo was taken. The MOSE barrier system, finished in 2020, was supposed to fix that.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress, Photochrom Prints Collection.

tweets.text:
The Grand Canal, Venice. Color print, 1895. Not painted — lithographic tints applied chemically to a photograph. A process invented in Zurich in 1888.

The gondolas here are working boats. Venice had about 10,000 of them for daily transport. Today: 400, all for tourists.

The buildings haven't moved. The water has. Venice floods 60 times a year now, up from 10 when this was taken.

Photochrom print, ~1895. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Constantinople from the Golden Horn, 1895
posts.text:
Constantinople from the Golden Horn, circa 1895. The city that would become Istanbul in 1930 but hadn't stopped being Constantinople yet.

The minarets are Suleymaniye Mosque, built 1550-1557 by the architect Sinan for Suleiman the Magnificent. Still standing. Most of what's around it is not.

In 1895 Constantinople was the capital of a dying empire. The Ottoman state was 600 years old and had maybe 25 years left. The waterfront you see here — wooden houses, fishing boats, market stalls — was mostly destroyed in fires and rebuilt in concrete during the early Republic.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress, Photochrom Prints Collection.

tweets.text:
Constantinople from the Golden Horn, ~1895. The city that would become Istanbul in 1930 but hadn't stopped being Constantinople yet.

The minarets: Suleymaniye Mosque, built 1557. Still standing. Most of what's around it is not.

The Ottoman Empire was 600 years old with maybe 25 left. The wooden waterfront — houses, boats, market stalls — mostly burned and rebuilt in concrete.

Photochrom print. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Melon Trader in Samarkand, 1905
posts.text:
A melon trader in Samarkand, 1905. Prokudin-Gorsky photographed him sitting cross-legged in a market that had been operating on that spot for 2,000 years.

Samarkand in 1905 was a Russian colonial city layered on top of a Timurid one. The Russians had taken it in 1868. They built European-style avenues next to the medieval bazaars and left the markets alone because they worked.

The man is selling Uzbek melons — probably the torpedo-shaped variety that Babur, the first Mughal emperor, once called the finest fruit in the world. In 1905 you could buy one for a few kopecks.

Sergey Prokudin-Gorsky, 1905. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Melon trader in Samarkand, 1905. Color photograph. Sitting cross-legged in a market that had been on that spot for 2,000 years.

The Russians took Samarkand in 1868. Built European avenues next to medieval bazaars. Left the markets alone because they worked.

The melons: probably the torpedo-shaped variety that Babur, the first Mughal emperor, once called the finest fruit in the world.

Sergey Prokudin-Gorsky, 1905. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Jaffa Gate, Jerusalem, 1895
posts.text:
Jaffa Gate, Jerusalem, circa 1895. The main western entrance to the Old City. Donkeys, market vendors, and a Thomas Cook tourist office crammed against 16th-century Ottoman stonework.

In 1895 Jerusalem was a small Ottoman provincial city of about 45,000 people. The Old City walls, built by Suleiman the Magnificent in the 1530s, still defined the entire urban area. Almost everyone lived inside them.

The Cook's office is the detail. British tourism had already arrived. The empire running the city had 25 years left. The open ground outside this gate would become the commercial center of modern West Jerusalem.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress, Photochrom Prints Collection.

tweets.text:
Jaffa Gate, Jerusalem, ~1895. Donkeys, market vendors, and a Thomas Cook tourist office crammed against 16th-century Ottoman walls.

Jerusalem in 1895: a small Ottoman city of 45,000. The Old City walls, built in the 1530s, still defined the entire urban area. Almost everyone lived inside them.

The Cook's office is the detail. British tourism arrived before the empire fell.

Photochrom print. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Shah-i-Zinda, Samarkand, 1911
posts.text:
The entrance to Shah-i-Zinda, Samarkand. Photographed by Prokudin-Gorsky between 1905 and 1915. Two men sit in a doorway framed by Timurid tilework that was already 500 years old.

Shah-i-Zinda is a necropolis. A street of mausoleums climbing a hill on the edge of Samarkand. The tilework — cobalt blue, turquoise, white — was laid by craftsmen working for Timur and his successors in the 14th and 15th centuries.

By 1905 Samarkand had been Russian for 37 years. The Russians photographed the tilework, measured it, wrote monographs about it. They did not restore it. Earthquakes and neglect had already taken pieces. Soviet restoration in the 1970s replaced some original tiles with modern copies.

The blue you see here is the original.

Sergey Prokudin-Gorsky. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Shah-i-Zinda, Samarkand. Two men in a doorway framed by Timurid tilework that was already 500 years old when Prokudin-Gorsky photographed it.

A necropolis. A street of mausoleums climbing a hill. The cobalt and turquoise laid by craftsmen working for Timur in the 1400s.

Soviet restoration in the 1970s replaced some original tiles with copies. The blue in this photo is the original.

Prokudin-Gorsky. Library of Congress.

posts.title: The Skuritskhali River, Caucasus, 1905
posts.text:
The Skuritskhali River near Orto-Batum, in the Caucasus mountains of present-day Georgia. 1905. The water is that green.

Prokudin-Gorsky photographed the Caucasus during the brief window when it was accessible by rail but not yet touched by Soviet industrialization. The forests here were old-growth. The villages were unchanged from the medieval period.

Within 30 years, Stalin — who grew up 200 miles from here — would collectivize the entire region. The villages were reorganized. The old-growth forest was partly logged for the war effort. The river still flows, but the world around it is different.

Sergey Prokudin-Gorsky, 1905. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
The Skuritskhali River, Caucasus, 1905. The water is that green.

Prokudin-Gorsky shot the Caucasus during the brief window when it was reachable by rail but untouched by Soviet industry. Old-growth forests. Medieval villages.

Within 30 years, Stalin — who grew up 200 miles away — would collectivize the entire region. The river still flows. The world around it is different.

Library of Congress.

posts.title: The Pyramids from the Nile Shore, Cairo, 1895
posts.text:
Bedouins resting near the Great Pyramid, Cairo, circa 1895. Photochrom print. The pyramids sit in open desert with no buildings nearby.

In 1895 the Giza plateau was a camel ride from Cairo. No paved roads. No Pizza Hut across the street. The city ended and the desert began, and the pyramids were in the desert.

Today Cairo's suburbs reach the base of the Sphinx. The last open ground between the city and the monuments is a parking lot. The view in this photo has not existed for decades.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Bedouins near the Great Pyramid, Cairo, ~1895. The pyramids sit in open desert. No buildings nearby.

In 1895, Giza was a camel ride from Cairo. No paved roads. The city ended and the desert began.

Today Cairo's suburbs reach the base of the Sphinx. This view hasn't existed for decades.

Photochrom print. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Old Cairo and the Citadel, 1895
posts.text:
Old Cairo and the Citadel of Saladin, circa 1895. The medieval city seen from the Nile bank. Low mud-brick buildings, minarets, the Citadel on its hill.

Saladin built the Citadel in 1176 to defend against the Crusaders. It served as Egypt's seat of government for 700 years. Muhammad Ali built his mosque on top in the 1840s — the one with the two pencil-thin minarets.

The low buildings in the foreground are the old Coptic and Islamic quarters. Most were demolished in the 20th century for road widening and modern development. The Citadel remains. The skyline around it does not.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Old Cairo and the Citadel of Saladin, ~1895. Medieval city from the Nile bank. Low mud-brick, minarets, the Citadel on its hill.

Saladin built it in 1176. Seat of government for 700 years. Muhammad Ali's mosque added in the 1840s.

The low buildings in front: old Coptic and Islamic quarters. Most demolished for road widening. The Citadel remains. The skyline does not.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Mosque of Sultan Barquq, Cairo, 1895
posts.text:
The Mosque of Sultan Barquq, Cairo, circa 1895. Built in 1386, this was the first Mamluk monument on what became the City of the Dead — Cairo's vast necropolis where people live among the tombs.

Barquq was a Circassian slave who became sultan of Egypt. His mosque and madrasa on al-Muizz Street was considered the finest in Cairo. The stone carving and marble inlay took six years.

In this photo the mosque sits among low buildings on a quiet street. Today al-Muizz Street is a pedestrian zone, heavily restored. The buildings around the mosque have been replaced. The mosque itself survived because the Egyptian government listed it in 1881.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Mosque of Sultan Barquq, Cairo, ~1895. Built 1386. First Mamluk monument on what became the City of the Dead.

Barquq was a Circassian slave who became sultan. His mosque took six years of stone carving and marble inlay.

The buildings around it: replaced. The mosque survived because Egypt listed it in 1881.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: The Harbor at Algiers, 1899
posts.text:
The harbor and admiralty at Algiers, circa 1899. The French colonial waterfront, white buildings climbing the hill behind it. Algiers was called 'la Ville Blanche' — the White City.

France invaded Algiers in 1830 and stayed 132 years. They demolished the lower Casbah to build French-style boulevards, banks, and a harbor. The Ottoman waterfront vanished under French stone.

The upper Casbah survived because the French couldn't build on the steep terrain. It's still there — a UNESCO site since 1992. But the harbor in this photo is now a container port. The admiralty building was repurposed after independence in 1962.

Photochrom print, circa 1899. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
The harbor at Algiers, ~1899. French colonial waterfront, white buildings climbing the hill. 'La Ville Blanche.'

France invaded in 1830, stayed 132 years. Demolished the lower Casbah for boulevards and banks. The Ottoman waterfront vanished.

The upper Casbah survived — UNESCO since 1992. The harbor is now a container port.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Street of the Camels, Algiers, 1899
posts.text:
The Street of the Camels in the Casbah, Algiers, circa 1899. A narrow lane in the old Ottoman quarter, buildings leaning over the street, laundry hanging between walls.

The Casbah of Algiers had about 1,800 houses, 60 mosques, and 100 fountains when the French arrived in 1830. The French systematically demolished the lower half to build their colonial city.

The Battle of Algiers in 1957 was fought in these streets. French paratroopers went house to house. The damage was extensive. After independence in 1962, the Casbah fell into neglect. Today about a third of the original structures remain.

Photochrom print, circa 1899. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Street of the Camels, Casbah, Algiers, ~1899. Narrow lanes, buildings leaning over the street, laundry between walls.

The Casbah had 1,800 houses, 60 mosques, 100 fountains in 1830. The French demolished the lower half for their colonial city.

The Battle of Algiers in 1957 was fought here. Today about a third of the original structures remain.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Algiers Harbor by Moonlight, 1899
posts.text:
Algiers harbor at night, circa 1899. A photochrom print with lithographic tints simulating moonlight on the water. Ships at anchor. The white city glowing on the hill.

This is one of the few photochrom prints that attempts a night scene. The Zurich firm Orell Fussli, which invented the process, created moonlight effects by adjusting the tint layers. The result is half photograph, half painting.

The harbor was the arrival point for millions of French settlers — pieds-noirs — who came to Algeria between 1830 and 1962. When Algeria won independence, about 800,000 of them left in a few months. The harbor saw both arrivals.

Photochrom print, circa 1899. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Algiers harbor at night, ~1899. Photochrom with lithographic tints simulating moonlight. Ships at anchor. The white city glowing.

One of the few photochrom night scenes. Created by adjusting tint layers in Zurich. Half photograph, half painting.

This harbor was the arrival point for pieds-noirs settlers — and their departure when 800,000 left in 1962.

Library of Congress.

posts.title: The Souk el-Trouk, Tunis, 1899
posts.text:
Souk el-Trouk (Market of the Turks) in the Tunis medina, circa 1899. Covered market, striped arches, merchants sitting in doorways.

The Tunis medina dates to the 7th century. By the 1890s it had over 700 monuments — mosques, madrasas, palaces, fountains — packed into a few square kilometers. The French protectorate (1881-1956) left the medina alone and built a European city next to it.

The souk in this photo still exists, technically. The structure is there. But the goods changed from spices and textiles to tourist souvenirs. The merchants changed. The customers changed. The building survived. The culture inside it didn't.

Photochrom print, circa 1899. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Souk el-Trouk, Tunis, ~1899. Covered market, striped arches, merchants in doorways.

The Tunis medina dates to the 7th century. 700 monuments in a few square km. The French left the medina alone and built next to it.

The souk still exists. The structure is there. But spices became souvenirs. The building survived. The culture inside it didn't.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Moorish Cafe, Tunis, 1899
posts.text:
A Moorish cafe in the Tunis medina, circa 1899. Men sitting on low platforms, drinking coffee, smoking. Tiled walls, arched doorways.

Coffee arrived in Tunis from Yemen in the 16th century. By the 1890s there were hundreds of these cafes — places where men met, talked politics, played chess, and smoked narghiles. They were living rooms for a city where houses were private.

The French colonial administration monitored the cafes as hotbeds of nationalism. They were right. The Neo-Destour independence movement organized partly through cafe networks. After independence in 1956, many traditional cafes were replaced by French-style bistros.

Photochrom print, circa 1899. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Moorish cafe, Tunis, ~1899. Men on low platforms. Coffee, smoke, tiled walls.

Coffee reached Tunis from Yemen in the 1500s. Hundreds of these cafes served as public living rooms. Chess, politics, narghiles.

The French monitored them as hotbeds of nationalism. They were right. The independence movement organized through cafe networks.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Hagia Sophia Entrance, Constantinople, 1895
posts.text:
The entrance to Hagia Sophia, Constantinople, circa 1895. Still a mosque at this point. It had been a mosque for 442 years, a church for 916 years before that.

Built in 537 by Justinian. For a thousand years it was the largest enclosed space in the world. The dome is 31 meters across. When the Ottomans took Constantinople in 1453, Sultan Mehmed II converted it to a mosque. The minarets were added over the next century.

In 1935 Ataturk turned it into a museum. In 2020 Erdogan turned it back into a mosque. The building has outlived three empires and will probably outlive a few more.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Hagia Sophia entrance, Constantinople, ~1895. A mosque for 442 years at this point. A church for 916 years before that.

Built 537. Largest enclosed space in the world for a thousand years. Dome: 31 meters across.

Museum in 1935. Mosque again in 2020. The building has outlived three empires.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Eminonu Bazaar and Yeni Cami, Constantinople, 1895
posts.text:
Yeni Cami mosque and the Eminonu market, Constantinople, circa 1895. The mosque is 'new' — built 1597-1665. Took 68 years because the architect, the sultan's mother, and two sultans all died during construction.

The market at its base is the Egyptian Bazaar (Misir Carsisi), built to fund the mosque. Spices from Egypt, India, China. The building still stands and still sells spices, but the market around it in this photo — the wooden stalls, the street vendors, the waterfront chaos — was cleared in the 20th century.

The Galata Bridge is visible in the background. It was rebuilt four times.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Yeni Cami and Eminonu market, Constantinople, ~1895. The 'new' mosque took 68 years to build (1597-1665). The architect, the sultan's mother, and two sultans all died during construction.

The Egyptian Bazaar at its base still sells spices. The wooden stalls and waterfront chaos around it: cleared.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: The Galata Bridge, Constantinople, 1895
posts.text:
The Galata Bridge over the Golden Horn, Constantinople, circa 1895. A pontoon bridge on floating barrels, connecting the old city to Pera (now Beyoglu). Boats, pedestrians, horse-drawn carts sharing the roadway.

This bridge was the fourth version. The first was built in 1845. Each one rotted or burned and was replaced. The bridge you see in this photo was replaced in 1912 with a German-engineered steel span, which itself was replaced in 1992.

The fishermen on the bridge are the one constant. People have fished off the Galata Bridge for 180 years and still do today.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
The Galata Bridge, Constantinople, ~1895. A pontoon bridge on floating barrels. Boats, pedestrians, horse carts.

This was the fourth version. First built 1845. Each one rotted or burned. Replaced in 1912, replaced again in 1992.

The fishermen are the one constant. People have fished off the Galata Bridge for 180 years.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Damascus and the Minaret of the Bride, 1895
posts.text:
Damascus from above, circa 1895. The Umayyad Mosque's Minaret of the Bride rises above flat-roofed houses. Damascus was already 4,000 years old when this photo was taken.

The Umayyad Mosque was built in 715 on the site of a Roman temple, which was built on the site of an Aramean temple. It contains what Muslims believe is the head of John the Baptist. The mosque has burned three times and been rebuilt each time.

The residential quarters in this photo were largely intact until the Syrian civil war. The Old City of Damascus sustained significant damage starting in 2012. Some of these streets no longer exist.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Damascus from above, ~1895. The Umayyad Mosque's Minaret of the Bride above flat-roofed houses. The city was already 4,000 years old.

The mosque was built in 715 on a Roman temple site, on an Aramean temple site. Burned three times. Rebuilt each time.

The residential quarters here were largely intact until the Syrian civil war. Some of these streets no longer exist.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: The Street Called Straight, Damascus, 1895
posts.text:
The Street Called Straight, Damascus, circa 1895. Mentioned in the Bible (Acts 9:11). Paul was taken to this street after his conversion on the road to Damascus. It's been continuously inhabited for over 3,000 years.

In this photo it's a covered market — fabric awnings over a Roman-era road. The shops sell textiles, copper, soap. The street is about 1,500 meters long, running east-west through the old city.

The eastern gate is visible. It's Roman — 2nd century AD. The shops and awnings around it changed constantly for millennia. The street itself, the path worn into the stone, has not moved.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
The Street Called Straight, Damascus, ~1895. Mentioned in the Bible (Acts 9:11). Paul was taken here after his conversion. Continuously inhabited for 3,000+ years.

A covered market in a Roman-era road. Textiles, copper, soap. 1,500 meters east to west.

The eastern gate: Roman, 2nd century AD. The street itself hasn't moved.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Beirut and Mount Lebanon, 1895
posts.text:
Beirut from above, circa 1895. Red-roofed houses, church steeples, Mount Lebanon snow-capped in the distance. A small Mediterranean port town of about 120,000.

In 1895 Beirut was a cosmopolitan Ottoman city — Muslims, Christians, Druze, Jews. The American University was 30 years old. The city was known for printing, education, and silk trade.

Beirut would survive the Ottoman collapse, the French mandate, independence in 1943, a devastating civil war (1975-1990), and the 2020 port explosion that destroyed much of the waterfront. The red roofs in this photo are mostly gone. The mountains remain.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Beirut from above, ~1895. Red roofs, church steeples, Mount Lebanon snow-capped behind. A port town of 120,000.

Cosmopolitan Ottoman city. Muslims, Christians, Druze, Jews. Known for printing and silk.

Would survive: Ottoman collapse, French mandate, civil war (1975-1990), the 2020 port explosion. The red roofs are mostly gone. The mountains remain.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: The Forum, Pompeii, 1895
posts.text:
The Forum of Pompeii, circa 1895. Vesuvius in the background. This was the center of public life until August 24, 79 AD.

Pompeii had about 11,000 residents. The eruption buried the city under 4 to 6 meters of volcanic ash. It stayed buried for 1,700 years until excavation began in 1748.

In this photo, the Forum has been partially excavated but much of the city is still underground. The columns are stumps. The temples are foundations. Everything above two meters was crushed by the weight of the ash.

Vesuvius last erupted in 1944. Three million people live in its shadow today.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
The Forum, Pompeii, ~1895. Vesuvius in the background. Center of public life until August 24, 79 AD.

11,000 residents. Buried under 4-6 meters of ash. Stayed buried 1,700 years.

In this photo the Forum is partially excavated, much of the city still underground. Vesuvius last erupted in 1944. Three million people live in its shadow.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Street of the Tombs, Pompeii, 1895
posts.text:
The Street of the Tombs, Pompeii, circa 1895. The main road leading out of the city, lined with the monuments of wealthy families. Romans buried their dead outside city walls — the closer to the gate, the higher the status.

The tombs date from the 1st century BC. Some held cremation urns. Others had stone couches for funeral banquets. The families whose names are carved here — the Istacidii, the Mammii — were Pompeii's elite.

This was the first street excavated, starting in 1763. The Grand Tourists of the 18th and 19th centuries walked this road. Byron came here. Shelley came here. Goethe came here.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Street of the Tombs, Pompeii, ~1895. Main road out of the city, lined with monuments of wealthy families. Romans buried dead outside walls — closer to the gate, higher the status.

First street excavated, 1763. Byron walked here. Shelley walked here. Goethe walked here.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: St. Basil's and Red Square, Moscow, 1895
posts.text:
St. Basil's Cathedral and the Upper Trading Rows, Moscow, circa 1895. The Trading Rows are the long building on the left — what would become GUM department store under the Soviets.

Ivan the Terrible built St. Basil's in 1555-1561 to celebrate the conquest of Kazan. Legend says he blinded the architects so they could never build anything better. The legend is probably false.

The Upper Trading Rows were rebuilt in 1893 in a Russian Revival style — the building in this photo is brand new. It had 1,200 shops under a glass-and-steel roof. After the Revolution it became a government building. Stalin considered demolishing it. It survived.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
St. Basil's and the Upper Trading Rows, Moscow, ~1895. The Trading Rows: 1,200 shops under a glass-and-steel roof. Brand new in this photo.

Ivan the Terrible built St. Basil's in 1555-1561 after conquering Kazan. Legend says he blinded the architects.

After the Revolution the Trading Rows became a government building. Stalin considered demolishing them.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Franz Joseph Bridge, Budapest, 1895
posts.text:
The Franz Joseph Bridge connecting Buda and Pest, circa 1895. Named after the Austro-Hungarian emperor. Horse-drawn trams crossing the Danube.

Budapest was two cities until 1873 — Buda on the western hills, Pest on the eastern flat. The bridges stitched them together. By 1895 there were four.

This bridge was destroyed in World War II. The retreating Germans blew up every bridge in Budapest in January 1945. All of them. The replacement bridge, built in 1946, was renamed Liberty Bridge.

The horse trams in this photo ran until 1928. Budapest's electric tram system, which replaced them, is now the oldest continuously operating tram network in the world.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Franz Joseph Bridge, Budapest, ~1895. Horse trams crossing the Danube. Named after the Austro-Hungarian emperor.

Buda and Pest were two cities until 1873. The bridges stitched them together.

The retreating Germans blew up every bridge in Budapest in January 1945. All of them. This one was rebuilt as Liberty Bridge.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: The Old Town Bridge Tower, Prague, 1895
posts.text:
The Old Town Bridge Tower in Prague, circa 1895. Gateway to the Charles Bridge. Built in 1380 by Peter Parler, the same architect who built St. Vitus Cathedral.

The tower was the entry point to the bridge and the city. In 1621, after the Battle of White Mountain, the Habsburgs displayed the severed heads of 12 Czech nobles on the tower. They stayed there for 10 years.

Prague in 1895 was part of Austria-Hungary. It would become the capital of Czechoslovakia in 1918, be occupied by Germany in 1939, liberated in 1945, taken by the Soviets, and become the Czech Republic in 1993. The tower watched all of it.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Old Town Bridge Tower, Prague, ~1895. Gateway to the Charles Bridge. Built 1380.

In 1621 the Habsburgs displayed 12 severed heads of Czech nobles here. They stayed 10 years.

Prague in 1895: Austria-Hungary. Then Czechoslovakia, German occupation, Soviet control, Czech Republic. The tower watched all of it.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: The Astronomical Clock, Prague, 1895
posts.text:
The Prague Astronomical Clock (Orloj), circa 1895. Installed on the Old Town Hall in 1410. It's been running for 615 years.

The clock shows three things: the position of the sun and moon in the sky, a calendar dial with zodiac signs, and a procession of the 12 apostles that appears on the hour. Below it are figures of Vanity, Greed, Death, and a Turkish invader.

The Nazis nearly destroyed it in May 1945 when they set fire to the Old Town Hall during the Prague Uprising. The building burned. The clock was damaged and took two years to repair. The figures of the apostles had to be recarved.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
The Prague Astronomical Clock, ~1895. Installed 1410. Running for 615 years.

Shows sun and moon positions, zodiac calendar, 12 apostles on the hour. Below: Vanity, Greed, Death, and a Turkish invader.

The Nazis set fire to the Old Town Hall in May 1945. The clock was damaged. The apostles had to be recarved.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: The Colosseum Interior, Rome, 1895
posts.text:
Inside the Colosseum, Rome, circa 1895. The underground chambers exposed. These hypogea held gladiators, prisoners, and animals before they were lifted to the arena floor by wooden elevators.

The Colosseum seated 50,000. It opened in 80 AD with 100 days of games. An estimated 400,000 people and a million animals died in the arena over its four centuries of operation.

In this photo, grass grows on the arena floor and the cross of a Via Crucis station is visible — the Colosseum was consecrated as a Christian site in the 18th century. The excavation of the underground had just begun.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Inside the Colosseum, Rome, ~1895. Underground chambers exposed. These held gladiators and animals before wooden elevators lifted them to the arena.

Seated 50,000. Opened 80 AD. An estimated 400,000 people died here over four centuries.

Grass on the arena floor. A Via Crucis cross. Consecrated as a Christian site in the 1700s.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: The Forum Romano, Rome, 1895
posts.text:
The Roman Forum, circa 1895. This was the center of the Roman Republic and Empire — law courts, temples, markets, the Senate house. The most powerful half-acre on Earth for 500 years.

After the fall of Rome the Forum was used as a cattle pasture. For a thousand years it was called Campo Vaccino — Cow Field. Cows grazed among the columns of the Temple of Saturn.

Excavation began in the 1800s. In this photo, the ruins are partly cleared but still surrounded by earth and medieval buildings. The full excavation wouldn't be complete until the 1930s.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
The Roman Forum, ~1895. Center of the Republic and Empire. Law courts, temples, Senate house. Most powerful half-acre on Earth for 500 years.

After Rome fell, it became a cattle pasture. For a thousand years: Campo Vaccino. Cows among the columns of the Temple of Saturn.

Full excavation not complete until the 1930s.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Castel Sant'Angelo, Rome, 1895
posts.text:
Castel Sant'Angelo and its bridge, Rome, circa 1895. Originally built as Emperor Hadrian's mausoleum in 139 AD. It's been a fortress, a papal residence, a prison, and a museum. The same building, five different jobs.

The angel on top was added in 1753 to commemorate the end of a plague in 590 AD — Pope Gregory the Great saw an angel sheathing a sword over the building, meaning the plague was over.

The bridge in front was built by Hadrian in 134 AD. The angel statues were added by Bernini in 1669. The bridge has been crossed by pilgrims, armies, prisoners on their way to execution, and now tourists.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Castel Sant'Angelo, Rome, ~1895. Built as Hadrian's mausoleum in 139 AD. Then a fortress, papal residence, prison, museum. Same building, five jobs.

The angel: added 1753 for a plague that ended in 590 AD. Pope Gregory saw an angel sheathing a sword.

The bridge: Hadrian, 134 AD. The angels: Bernini, 1669.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Narrow Streets of Naples, 1895
posts.text:
A narrow street in Naples, circa 1895. Laundry overhead. Vendors on the ground floor. Life happening vertically — six stories of families stacked above the street, their lives visible through open windows.

Naples in 1895 had recently survived a cholera epidemic (1884) that killed 7,000 people. The Italian government responded by demolishing the worst slums and building wide boulevards. The 'Risanamento' — the cleansing — destroyed entire neighborhoods.

Streets like this one survived because they were outside the demolition zone. Similar streets still exist in the Quartieri Spagnoli. But the life in the photo — the horse carts, the street vendors, the open-air economy — is mostly gone.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Naples, ~1895. Laundry overhead, vendors below. Life vertical — six stories of families stacked above the street.

Naples had just survived an 1884 cholera epidemic (7,000 dead). The government demolished the worst slums — the 'Risanamento.' Entire neighborhoods erased.

Streets like this survived in the Quartieri Spagnoli. The buildings remain. The life in the photo mostly doesn't.

Photochrom. Library of Congress.

posts.title: The Macaroni Seller, Naples, 1895
posts.text:
A macaroni seller on a Naples street, circa 1895. He's cooking pasta outdoors, selling portions to workers and children. Fast food, 1890s.

Naples invented street food. Pasta vendors like this one served the poor — factory workers, dock laborers, the unemployed. You ate standing up, with your hands, from a paper cone. No fork, no plate. A portion cost two centesimi.

The Neapolitan street food economy lasted centuries. Pizza margherita, fried dough, seafood stands — all born on streets like this. The street vendors were gradually pushed out by health regulations in the 20th century.

Photochrom print, circa 1895. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:

You ate standing, with your hands, from a paper cone. Two centesimi.

posts.title: Medrese Divan-Begi, Bukhara, 1911
posts.text:
The Divan-Begi Madrasah at Lyabi-Khauz square, Bukhara. Photographed by Prokudin-Gorsky, circa 1911. Built in 1622, it was originally a caravanserai — an inn for Silk Road merchants — before the emir converted it to a religious school.

The facade has two mosaic phoenixes carrying white does toward a sun with a human face. Islamic art traditionally avoids depicting living creatures. The architect got away with it because the building wasn't originally religious.

Bukhara was the intellectual capital of Central Asia for 900 years. The Bolsheviks took it in 1920. The madrasah stopped being a school. Soviet 'restoration' in the 1960s replaced much of the original tilework.

Prokudin-Gorsky. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Divan-Begi Madrasah, Bukhara, ~1911. Built 1622 as a Silk Road inn, converted to a religious school.

The facade has mosaic phoenixes carrying does toward a sun with a human face. Islamic art avoids living creatures. The architect got away with it because the building wasn't originally religious.

The Bolsheviks took Bukhara in 1920. Soviet restoration in the 1960s replaced much of the original tile.

Prokudin-Gorsky. Library of Congress.

posts.title: The Emir's Summer Palace, Bukhara, 1911
posts.text:
The summer palace of the Emir of Bukhara, circa 1911. Prokudin-Gorsky photographed the palace grounds — ornamental gardens, pavilions, a mix of Russian and Central Asian architectural styles.

The Emir Alim Khan built this to impress visiting Russian officials. It had a harem, a guest house, a water pavilion, and a reception hall with mirrored ceilings. The gardens were irrigated by a system that diverted river water.

When the Bolsheviks invaded in 1920, the emir fled and the palace was looted. The Red Army used it as a barracks. It's now a museum, but the gardens and most of the interiors are gone.

Prokudin-Gorsky. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Summer palace of the Emir of Bukhara, ~1911. Ornamental gardens, pavilions, mirrored ceilings. Built to impress visiting Russian officials.

When the Bolsheviks invaded in 1920, the emir fled. The palace was looted. The Red Army used it as barracks.

Now a museum. The gardens and most interiors: gone.

Prokudin-Gorsky. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Sentry at the Emir's Palace, Bukhara, 1911
posts.text:
A sentry at the Emir of Bukhara's palace with old cannons, circa 1911. Prokudin-Gorsky found this guard standing among weapons from a previous century — decorative, not functional. The emirate's real military power was gone.

Bukhara in 1911 was a Russian protectorate. The emir had a palace and a title but no army, no foreign policy, no real power. Russia controlled the borders, the railroads, the cotton trade.

The guard's uniform is traditional Bukharan. The cannons are probably 18th century. The whole scene is a performance of sovereignty that had already ended. Nine years later the Bolsheviks dropped the pretense.

Prokudin-Gorsky. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:

Bukhara in 1911: a Russian protectorate. The emir had a title but no army, no foreign policy.

The whole scene is a performance of sovereignty that had already ended. Nine years later the Bolsheviks dropped the pretense.

posts.title: Village of Arakani, Dagestan, 1904
posts.text:
The village of Arakani in Dagestan, circa 1904. Prokudin-Gorsky photographed this mountain settlement clinging to a cliff face in the Caucasus. Stone houses stacked on top of each other, the roof of one house serving as the courtyard of the house above.

Dagestan means 'land of mountains.' It has more ethnic groups and languages per square mile than almost anywhere on Earth — over 30 distinct peoples in an area smaller than West Virginia.

Arakani was on the route of Imam Shamil's resistance against the Russian Empire in the 1830s-1860s. The Russians eventually took it. Soviet collectivization forced many mountain villagers into the lowlands. Some villages were abandoned entirely.

Prokudin-Gorsky. Library of Congress.

tweets.text:
Arakani, Dagestan, ~1904. A village clinging to a cliff. Stone houses stacked — one roof is the next courtyard.

Dagestan: 'land of mountains.' Over 30 ethnic groups in an area smaller than West Virginia.

On the route of Imam Shamil's resistance against Russia (1830s-1860s). Soviet collectivization later forced many villagers into the lowlands.

Prokudin-Gorsky. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Shamil's Gates, Gunib, Dagestan, 1904
posts.text:
Shamil's Gates at Upper Gunib, Dagestan, circa 1904. This narrow passage in the rock is where Imam Shamil, leader of the Caucasian resistance, made his last stand against the Russian army in 1859.

Shamil held off the Russian Empire for 25 years with guerrilla warfare in these mountains. He united dozens of Dagestani and Chechen tribes under one command. The Russians sent 200,000 troops. Shamil had maybe 5,000.

He surrendered at Gunib on September 6, 1859. The Russians treated him with respect — he was exiled to Kaluga, then allowed to make the Hajj to Mecca. He died in Medina in 1871.

Prokudin-Gorsky. Library of Congress.
